narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built an impressive academic profile with a standout undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 37, right around the norm of 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, and come from an upper-middle-class family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.1%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize applicants with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is below the average of about 5.5; this suggests your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower part of your class compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Counterbalancing that, your high undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the model toward expecting you to pass by showing exceptional overall college achievement far exceeding the norm. Your family's upper-middle income also helped, nudging the prediction in a favorable direction, as students from similar backgrounds often perform well. While your Year 1 decile of 1 was another early weak point, it wasn't as decisive as your Year 3 standing in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile stacks up strong against averages in GPA and LSAT, but the dip in later-year class rankings stood out as the top concern when compared to students who typically pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive—it's the model weighing patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year decile rankings, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore schools with holistic reviews that value your GPA strength. With your solid foundation, targeted improvements can open doors—keep pushing, and reapply stronger.
